s: Scorched handprints and singed books: the Church's case for Purgatory
w: Tucked into a side room of Sacro Cuore del Suffragio, this miniature museum displays objects Father Victor Jouet gathered after an 1897 fire scorched what he believed was a face into the sacristy wall. Handprints on books, night-shirts, and prayer cards — each supposedly pressed by a soul begging prayers from the living. Barely signed, never crowded.
_facts: Founded by Father Victor Jouet after a 1897 fire he interpreted as a supernatural visitation
Located in the neo-Gothic Sacro Cuore del Suffragio church on Lungotevere Prati
Displays objects with scorch marks attributed to souls in Purgatory seeking intercession
Collection includes scorched books, nightshirts, and prayer cards with alleged provenance labels

s: Nero's golden palace, buried under the Colosseum's foundations
w: After the great fire of 64 AD, Nero seized 300 acres to build a gilded pleasure complex. His successors piled the Colosseum and the Baths of Titus on top within decades. Renaissance painters including Raphael dangled into the buried vaults to study the frescoes — giving us the word 'grotesque'. Reopened in the 1990s. Still being excavated.
_facts: Built by Nero after 64 AD fire, covering roughly 300 acres of central Rome
Demolished by Vespasian and Titus, who built the Colosseum on top
Raphael and Michelangelo studied the buried frescoes; the word 'grotesque' comes from 'grotte' (caves) used for these buried rooms
Reopened to the public in the 1990s and still undergoing excavation

s: Twelve hectares of terraced gardens climbing the Janiculum, mostly deserted
w: Laid out in 1883 on the Corsini palace gardens, the Sapienza University botanical garden climbs the Janiculum in terraces — a 17th-century Triton fountain, cactus house, Japanese garden, 3,500 species. A bamboo wood sits at the top; below it, a garden for the blind planted with aromatic species. One of the least-visited green spaces inside the Aurelian Walls.
_facts: Established 1883 on the grounds of Palazzo Corsini on the Janiculum slope
Operated by Sapienza University of Rome, covering 12 hectares
Contains roughly 3,500 plant species including a Japanese garden and a sensory garden for the blind
Features a 17th-century Fontana dei Tritoni

s: Two thousand years of Rome, stacked in one city block
w: A branch of the Museo Nazionale Romano built over a single insula excavated through every era: Roman portico, Lombard rubbish dump, medieval monastery, Renaissance workshops — all compressed into a few metres of soil. The displays sit above open trenches. Tells the story of Rome's transformation after the fall better than anywhere else. Almost always quiet.
_facts: Built on the portico of the Theatre of Balbus (13 BC)
Excavations found continuous occupation from the 1st century BC to the present
One of four sites of the Museo Nazionale Romano; opened 1981
Stratigraphy includes Roman, early medieval, monastic, and Renaissance layers

s: Eleven ancient columns still propping up Rome's Chamber of Commerce
w: Eleven Corinthian columns from Hadrian's temple — dedicated by Antoninus Pius in 145 AD on the Campus Martius — rise from the side wall of a 17th-century palazzo by Carlo Fontana in Piazza di Pietra. The building served as a papal customs house and later Rome's stock exchange. Now it is the Chamber of Commerce. Most tourists walk past without looking up.
_facts: Dedicated to the deified Hadrian by Antoninus Pius in 145 AD on the Campus Martius
Eleven columns and part of the cella wall survive, incorporated into a 17th-century building by Carlo Fontana
Used successively as a papal customs house and Rome's stock exchange (Borsa)
Now the Rome Chamber of Commerce; located in Piazza di Pietra

s: The last papal palace, now a museum of Rome's forgotten centuries
w: Built for the nephews of Pope Pius VI between 1791 and 1811 — the last great aristocratic palace raised in Rome before unification ended that world. Now houses the Museo di Roma: papal portraits, carnival scenes, papal carriages. The oval staircase with its lantern and columns alone justifies the entrance. Frequently overlooked beside Piazza Navona.
_facts: Built 1791-1811 for the Braschi family, nephews of Pope Pius VI; designed by Cosimo Morelli
The last major aristocratic palace built in Rome before Italian unification
Located between Piazza Navona, Campo de' Fiori, and Piazza di Pasquino
Now houses the Museo di Roma, covering medieval through 19th-century city history

s: Rome's first emperor built his tomb at 35; it took 2,000 years to reopen
w: Augustus began this circular mausoleum in 28 BC, still in his twenties. Marcellus, Agrippa, then Augustus himself were interred here. The tomb became a fortress, a vineyard, a concert hall; Mussolini cleared it for a fascist piazza. Closed for restoration for 14 years, it reopened in 2021. Every repurposing is Rome in miniature.
_facts: Commissioned by Augustus in 28 BC on the Campus Martius
First to be buried: Marcellus (23 BC), then Agrippa (12 BC), then Augustus (14 AD)
Repurposed as a fortress (Colonna family), a concert hall, and a Mussolini-era archaeological zone
Reopened to the public March 2021 after 14 years of restoration

s: Rome's second-largest baths, built for 8,000 daily bathers; Shelley wrote here
w: Built 212-216 AD under Caracalla, covering 11 hectares with hot, warm, and cold rooms, libraries, and exercise courts for over 8,000 bathers a day. The Goths cut the aqueducts in the 530s and they never reopened. Mithraic temples discovered in the basement rank among the largest Mithraea known. Shelley wrote Prometheus Unbound in these ruins in 1819.
_facts: Built 212-216 AD; Rome's second-largest thermae after the Baths of Diocletian
Served over 8,000 bathers daily across frigidarium, tepidarium, and caldarium
Fell into disuse in the 530s when Goths severed the aqueducts during the Gothic War
Mithraic temples in the basement rank among the largest Mithraea known
Shelley wrote parts of Prometheus Unbound here in 1819

s: Mussolini's private estate, built over Jewish catacombs, now a free public park
w: The Torlonia banking dynasty built this estate north of the centre; Mussolini rented it for one lira a year from 1925 to 1943. Below the park, 3rd and 4th-century Jewish catacombs — discovered in 1918, one of six known in Rome — run under the gardens. The park is now freely open; the Casino Nobile is a museum of its ambiguous glamour.
_facts: Mussolini rented the Casino Nobile from the Torlonia family for one lira per year (1925-1943)
Jewish catacombs beneath the villa date from the 3rd-4th centuries, discovered in 1918
One of six known Jewish catacomb complexes in Rome
Park opened to the public in 1978 and is freely accessible

s: Ring the bell; a nun will slide open a hatch and hand you the key
w: Founded 4th or 5th century, dedicated to four martyrs whose names were kept secret. After a Norman sack in 1084, the convent was rebuilt at half its original size. The secret is the Chapel of San Silvestro: ring the bell, a nun slides a hatch open, and you collect the key to 1246 AD frescoes depicting the Donation of Constantine in astonishing colour. The cosmatesque cloister is outside normal hours.
_facts: Founded 4th or 5th century, dedicated to four anonymous martyrs
Sacked and partially destroyed by Robert Guiscard's Normans in 1084; rebuilt on a reduced footprint
The Chapel of San Silvestro contains 1246 AD frescoes depicting the Donation of Constantine
Access requires ringing at the convent; nuns provide the key through a sliding hatch

n: Basilica di Santa Cecilia in Trastevere
s: The patron saint of music, buried under the church built on her house
w: Built over where Cecilia died in 230 AD after a failed beheading — three blows, no clean cut, three more days alive. Maderno's 1599 marble shows her as allegedly found when the tomb opened: on her side, neck bearing three cuts. Upstairs, on Tuesday and Sunday mornings only, Cavallini's 1293 Last Judgement fresco — one of Rome's finest — covers the entire choir wall.
_facts: Built over the house where Cecilia was believed to have died c. 230 AD
Present basilica rebuilt by Pope Paschal I in the 9th century
Stefano Maderno's marble sculpture (1599) depicts Cecilia as allegedly found with three neck wounds
Pietro Cavallini's Last Judgement fresco (1293) is accessible Tuesday and Sunday mornings only

s: Rome's only four-faced arch, misnamed for two thousand years
w: The only quadrifrons — a four-faced arch spanning a crossroads — preserved in Rome. Built in the early 4th century over the Cloaca Maxima in the Forum Boarium. 'Giano' has nothing to do with Janus: it is a medieval corruption of 'ianus', a Latin word for any covered passageway. The Frangipane family walled it up as a watchtower in the Middle Ages.
_facts: Only quadrifrons (four-faced) triumphal arch preserved in Rome
Built early 4th century AD over the Cloaca Maxima drain in the Forum Boarium
'Giano' is a medieval corruption of 'ianus', a Latin word for a covered passageway, not a reference to the god Janus
Used as a watchtower by the Frangipane family in the medieval period

s: Once the world's largest arena; now a grassy field with no seats
w: At peak use the Circus Maximus held 150,000 to 250,000 spectators for chariot racing between the Palatine and Aventine hills. At 621 metres long, it was the largest entertainment venue in the ancient world. After Rome's fall it was quarried until nothing remained above ground. Today it is an open park where the sheer emptiness is the exhibit.
_facts: At peak capacity held 150,000-250,000 spectators for chariot racing
Measured 621 m long and 118 m wide — the largest ancient entertainment venue
First documented use dates to the 6th century BC
Fell into disuse after the Gothic War; quarried into the Middle Ages until no structures remained above ground

s: A 1920s garden suburb built for workers, later used as a Fascist internal exile
w: Laid out from 1920 as a self-contained garden suburb for industrial workers — low-rise courtyard blocks, street theatres, communal laundries. In the 1930s the regime used it as a place of internal exile (confino), sending anti-fascists to live here under surveillance. It survived both eras with its community intact, and remains one of Rome's most characterful neighbourhoods.
_facts: Founded 18 February 1920 by the Istituto Autonomo Case Popolari as a garden suburb for workers
Designed in the English garden city tradition with communal courtyards and shared amenities
Used by the Fascist regime for internal exile (confino) of political opponents in the 1930s
Home to Teatro Palladium, now a cultural venue of Roma Tre University

s: Guillotines, thumbscrews, and papal punishment: Italy's museum of crime
w: Founded in 1931 inside the former Carceri Nuove prison (built 1655 on papal orders), this state museum traces Italian criminal justice from medieval torture through the Camorra. The collection includes a working guillotine used in the Papal States into the 1870s, stocks, branding irons, and 19th-century anthropological criminology displays. One of Rome's most overlooked specialist museums.
_facts: Founded 1931, housed in the former Carceri Nuove (New Prisons) built in 1655
Contains Papal States execution equipment including a guillotine used until 1870
Displays range from medieval torture instruments to early criminological anthropology
Located at Via del Gonfalone 29, rione Ponte, near Campo de' Fiori

s: A 9th-century chapel so encrusted with gold mosaic it was called paradise
w: The Chapel of San Zeno, completed 817 AD as a mausoleum for Pope Paschal I's mother, covers every surface — walls, vault, arch — in Byzantine gold mosaic. Its medieval nickname was 'Garden of Paradise'. A column in the entrance vestibule is venerated as a fragment of the column of Christ's flagellation, brought from Jerusalem. The basilica is half a block from Santa Maria Maggiore but rarely visited.
_facts: Present basilica built by Pope Paschal I in 817-824 AD
The Zeno Chapel (Cappella di San Zenone) was completed 817 AD as a mausoleum for Paschal's mother Theodora
Medieval nickname was 'Garden of Paradise' due to its total gold mosaic covering
Contains what is venerated as a fragment of the column of the Flagellation of Christ

s: Bramante's Renaissance cloister, now an art space with a very good cafe
w: Commissioned by Cardinal Carafa and completed c. 1500 by Bramante — working here at the same time as the Tempietto on the Janiculum. The two-storey cloister sits behind Santa Maria della Pace, known for Raphael's sibyl fresco. Upper gallery hosts contemporary art exhibitions; a cafe under the ground-floor arcade. One of few places in Rome to drink coffee inside a Renaissance masterpiece.
_facts: Commissioned by Cardinal Oliviero Carafa c. 1500; designed by Donato Bramante
Bramante also designed the Tempietto on the Janiculum during the same period
Attached to Santa Maria della Pace, which contains Raphael's fresco of the Sibyls (1514)
Now an art exhibition space with a cafe in the ground-floor arcade

s: An Italian senator's ancient sculpture collection, free in a Renaissance palazzo
w: Baron Giovanni Barracco spent decades collecting Egyptian, Assyrian, Cypriot, Greek, and Roman pieces on his travels as a member of parliament, donating them to Rome in 1902. Since 1948 the collection has been housed in the Renaissance Palazzetto Le Roy; the basement was extended over excavations of an ancient Roman domus. Admission free. On Corso Vittorio Emanuele but almost nobody enters.
_facts: Collection assembled by Giovanni Barracco (1829-1914), senator and art collector
Donated to Rome in 1902; housed in the Palazzetto Le Roy since 1948
Covers Egyptian, Assyrian, Phoenician, Cypriot, Etruscan, Greek, and Roman sculpture
Basement expanded over excavations of a Roman domus; admission free

s: Wax tableaux of 18th-century Rome, in a Carmelite convent off a quiet square
w: Established 1977 in a restored 17th-century Carmelite convent, originally as the Museum of Folklore and Romanesque Poets. The permanent collection reconstructs pre-modern Rome: workshops, street scenes, festival tableaux. Belli's manuscripts and Pinelli's prints of plebeian street life are here. The annual World Press Photo exhibition comes through. In a courtyard off Piazza Sant'Egidio — easy to miss.
_facts: Opened 1977 in a 17th-century Carmelite convent of Sant'Egidio; originally the Museum of Folklore
Permanent collection depicts popular Roman culture and daily life through the 19th century
Contains manuscripts of Roman dialect poet Giuseppe Gioachino Belli and prints by Bartolomeo Pinelli
Hosts the annual World Press Photo exhibition

n: Basilica di San Lorenzo fuori le Mura
s: A pilgrim basilica bombed in 1943; Pius XII came to the ruins that afternoon
w: One of Rome's seven pilgrim churches, built over the tomb of Lawrence, deacon and martyr, 258 AD. On 19 July 1943 US bombers targeting the adjacent rail yards hit the portico: photographs in the interior show the wreckage; the restored frescoes still show the join. Pius XII arrived that afternoon — a rare wartime departure from the Vatican. Pope Pius IX is buried in the portico.
_facts: One of Rome's Seven Pilgrim Churches; built over the tomb of Lawrence, martyred 258 AD
Pope Pius IX (1792-1878) is buried in the portico
Bombed by US Air Forces on 19 July 1943 when targeting the nearby Scalo Tiburtino railway yards
Pope Pius XII visited the ruins the same afternoon — a rare wartime departure from the Vatican

s: The Aventine's orange garden, with the best free panorama in Rome
w: Planted in the 1930s on the site of the medieval Savello fortress, this small public garden holds an unbroken terrace view across the Tiber to St Peter's dome, the Gianicolo, and the city rooftops. The Knights of Malta keyhole is a hundred metres away. The garden gets the morning light; the keyhole queue builds by mid-morning. Come here instead, or before.
_facts: Planted in the 1930s on the site of the medieval Savello family fortress on the Aventine Hill
Named for the bitter orange trees (aranci amari) that fill the garden
The terrace commands an unobstructed view across the Tiber towards St Peter's Basilica
Located about 100 metres from the famous Knights of Malta keyhole viewpoint

s: The world's oldest urban sewer, still draining Rome after 2,600 years
w: Begun under the Tarquin kings c. 600 BC to drain the marshes between Rome's hills, the Cloaca Maxima was later vaulted over. In 33 BC Agrippa reportedly rowed through it on an inspection tour. By the 1st century AD all eleven aqueducts were connected to flush it. Its arched mouth is still visible at Ponte Palatino. Parts remain in active use.
_facts: Begun under the Tarquinian kings c. 600 BC to drain marshes between Rome's hills
Agrippa conducted an inspection tour by boat in 33 BC and undertook major reconstruction
All eleven Roman aqueducts eventually connected to flush the sewer
The outfall arch is visible at Ponte Palatino; parts remain in active use today

n: Auditorium Parco della Musica Ennio Morricone
s: Renzo Piano's beetle-shell concert halls, built over a Bronze Age village
w: Opened 2002 on a site where workers immediately hit a Bronze Age village; the finds are displayed in an outdoor archaeological zone. Piano's three interlocking halls plus an 8,000-seat outdoor amphitheatre form the home of the Accademia Nazionale di Santa Cecilia. The acoustic design draws comparisons to Vienna and Amsterdam. Renamed for Ennio Morricone in 2021.
_facts: Opened 2002, designed by Renzo Piano; renamed for Ennio Morricone after his death in 2021
Construction uncovered a Bronze Age village; the finds are displayed in an outdoor archaeological area
Three concert halls (Sala Santa Cecilia, Sala Sinopoli, Sala Petrassi) plus outdoor Cavea (8,000 seats)
Home to the Accademia Nazionale di Santa Cecilia; acoustic design by Jurgen Reinhold of Muller-BBM

s: A Roman ruin lurking unannounced in Rome's largest piazza
w: In the gardens at the centre of Piazza Vittorio Emanuele II — the 1880s residential hub of the new capital — a ruined Roman brick structure identified as a 3rd-century nymphaeum of Alexander Severus sits largely unlabelled. Adjacent is the Porta Magica: a 17th-century Baroque doorway carved with alchemical symbols by the Marquis Palombara. Two stone giants flank the arch. Most visitors to the market walk straight past both.
_facts: Identified as a nymphaeum from the reign of Alexander Severus (222-235 AD)
Located in the central gardens of Piazza Vittorio Emanuele II, built in the 1880s
Adjacent to the Porta Magica, a 17th-century door with alchemical inscriptions by Marquis Massimiliano Palombara
The surrounding piazza hosts Rome's most diverse international food market

s: Rome's vast exhibition hall, opened to controversy in 1883 and never quietened
w: Pio Piacentini's neoclassical hall on Via Nazionale was immediately controversial when it opened in 1883 — too large, too classical. It served as polling station, army barracks, wartime shelter, and cinema before reopening as a museum in 1990. Three arches, columns, and a pediment announce it; inside, changing exhibitions, a cinema, and Rome's only rooftop restaurant.
_facts: Designed by Pio Piacentini, opened 1883 for the National Art Exhibition
Controversially large and classical for the newly-unified city
Repurposed as a polling station, army barracks, and wartime shelter over the 20th century
Restored and reopened as a multi-use cultural centre in 1990; houses a cinema and rooftop restaurant

s: A former sugar refinery, now Rome's leading experimental theatre
w: Opened 1999 by the Teatro di Roma in a decommissioned 1908 sugar refinery on the Ostiense bank of the Tiber. The conversion kept the raw industrial fabric: exposed brick, metal catwalks, concrete floors. It stages experimental theatre, contemporary dance, and international productions. The surrounding Ostiense waterfront, once solidly industrial, now anchors Rome's most lively after-dark district.
_facts: Opened 1999 by Teatro di Roma in a former sugar refinery (stabilimento zuccheriero) built 1908
Industrial conversion preserved exposed brick, metal catwalks, and concrete floors
Hosts experimental theatre, contemporary dance, and international productions
Located on Lungotevere Vittorio Gassman in the Ostiense/Testaccio industrial waterfront
